questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The masses of 10 kg and 20 kg , respectively, are connected by a massless spring, as shown in the figure. A force of 200 N acts on the 20 kg mass. At the instant shown, the 10 kg mass has acceleration 12 m s - 2 . What is the acceleration of 20 kg mass?

questions.question: The masses of 10 kg and 20 kg , respectively, are connected by a massless spring, as shown in the figure. A force of 200 N acts on the 20 kg mass. At the instant shown, the 10 kg mass has acceleration 12 m s - 2 . What is the acceleration of 20 kg mass?
questions.prompt: The masses of 10 kg and 20 kg , respectively, are connected by a massless spring, as shown in the figure. A force of 200 N acts on the 20 kg mass. At the instant shown, the 10 kg mass has acceleration 12 m s - 2 . What is the acceleration of 20 kg mass?
questions.html: The masses of 10 kg and 20 kg, respectively, are connected by a massless spring, as shown in the figure. A force of 200 N acts on the 20 kg mass. At the instant shown, the 10 kg mass has acceleration 12 m s-2. What is the acceleration of 20 kg mass?
questions.questionHtml: The masses of 10 kg and 20 kg, respectively, are connected by a massless spring, as shown in the figure. A force of 200 N acts on the 20 kg mass. At the instant shown, the 10 kg mass has acceleration 12 m s-2. What is the acceleration of 20 kg mass?
questions.solutionText: Solution From the free body diagram for the block of mass 10 Kg as it has acceleration 12 m s - 2 which is because of spring force F s = k x . where, K is spring constant and x is the extension of spring. So, F s = 10 a 2 ⇒ F s = 10 × 12 = 120 N ∵ a 2 = 12 m s - 2 Hence, spring applies 120 N force on it. Therefore, spring will apply 120 N of force on 20 kg of mass in the backward direction. Then, ∴ The net forward force on 20 kg mass = 200 - 120 = 80 N . Hence, acceleration of 20 kg is ∵ a = F net M ∴ a 1 = 80 20 = 4 m s - 2
questions.solution: Solution From the free body diagram for the block of mass 10 Kg as it has acceleration 12 m s - 2 which is because of spring force F s = k x . where, K is spring constant and x is the extension of spring. So, F s = 10 a 2 ⇒ F s = 10 × 12 = 120 N ∵ a 2 = 12 m s - 2 Hence, spring applies 120 N force on it. Therefore, spring will apply 120 N of force on 20 kg of mass in the backward direction. Then, ∴ The net forward force on 20 kg mass = 200 - 120 = 80 N . Hence, acceleration of 20 kg is ∵ a = F net M ∴ a 1 = 80 20 = 4 m s - 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

From the free body diagram for the block of mass 10 Kg as it has acceleration 12 m s-2 which is because of spring force Fs=kx.

where, K is spring constant and x is the extension of spring.

So,

Fs=10a2

⇒Fs=10×12=120 N ∵a2=12 m s-2

Hence, spring applies 120 N force on it.

Therefore, spring will apply 120 N of force on 20 kg of mass in the backward direction.

Then,

∴ The net forward force on 20 kg mass=200-120=80 N.

Hence, acceleration of 20 kg is

∵ a=FnetM

∴ a1=8020=4 m s-2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A crown glass prism of refracting angle 6 ° is to be used for deviation without dispersion with a flint glass of angle of prism α . Given: for crown glass μ r = 1.513 and μ v = 1.523 , for flint glass μ r = 1.645 and μ v = 1.665 . Find α
questions.questionText: A crown glass prism of refracting angle 6 ° is to be used for deviation without dispersion with a flint glass of angle of prism α . Given: for crown glass μ r = 1.513 and μ v = 1.523 , for flint glass μ r = 1.645 and μ v = 1.665 . Find α
questions.question: A crown glass prism of refracting angle 6 ° is to be used for deviation without dispersion with a flint glass of angle of prism α . Given: for crown glass μ r = 1.513 and μ v = 1.523 , for flint glass μ r = 1.645 and μ v = 1.665 . Find α
questions.prompt: A crown glass prism of refracting angle 6 ° is to be used for deviation without dispersion with a flint glass of angle of prism α . Given: for crown glass μ r = 1.513 and μ v = 1.523 , for flint glass μ r = 1.645 and μ v = 1.665 . Find α
questions.html: A crown glass prism of refracting angle 6° is to be used for deviation without dispersion with a flint glass of angle of prism α. Given: for crown glass μr=1.513 and μv=1.523 , for flint glass μr=1.645 and μv=1.665. Find α
questions.questionHtml: A crown glass prism of refracting angle 6° is to be used for deviation without dispersion with a flint glass of angle of prism α. Given: for crown glass μr=1.513 and μv=1.523 , for flint glass μr=1.645 and μv=1.665. Find α
questions.solutionText: Solution For deviation without dispersion, the net angular dispersion must be zero: ( μ v − μ r ) crown ⋅ A = ( μ v − μ r ) flint ⋅ α Crown glass: μ v − μ r = 1.523 − 1.513 = 0.010 Flint glass: μ v − μ r = 1.665 − 1.645 = 0.020 0.010 × 6 ° = 0.020 × α α = 0.010 × 6 ° 0.020 = 3 ° Correct option: (1) 3 °
questions.solution: Solution For deviation without dispersion, the net angular dispersion must be zero: ( μ v − μ r ) crown ⋅ A = ( μ v − μ r ) flint ⋅ α Crown glass: μ v − μ r = 1.523 − 1.513 = 0.010 Flint glass: μ v − μ r = 1.665 − 1.645 = 0.020 0.010 × 6 ° = 0.020 × α α = 0.010 × 6 ° 0.020 = 3 ° Correct option: (1) 3 °
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For deviation without dispersion, the net angular dispersion must be zero:
(μv−μr)crown⋅A=(μv−μr)flint⋅α

Crown glass: μv−μr=1.523−1.513=0.010
Flint glass: μv−μr=1.665−1.645=0.020

0.010×6°=0.020×α
α=0.010×6°0.020=3°

Correct option: (1) 3°

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: If p is the magnitude of linear momentum of a particle executing a uniform circular motion, then the ratio of centripetal force acting on the particle to its linear momentum is given by,
questions.questionText: If p is the magnitude of linear momentum of a particle executing a uniform circular motion, then the ratio of centripetal force acting on the particle to its linear momentum is given by,
questions.question: If p is the magnitude of linear momentum of a particle executing a uniform circular motion, then the ratio of centripetal force acting on the particle to its linear momentum is given by,
questions.prompt: If p is the magnitude of linear momentum of a particle executing a uniform circular motion, then the ratio of centripetal force acting on the particle to its linear momentum is given by,
questions.html: If p is the magnitude of linear momentum of a particle executing a uniform circular motion, then the ratio of centripetal force acting on the particle to its linear momentum is given by,
questions.questionHtml: If p is the magnitude of linear momentum of a particle executing a uniform circular motion, then the ratio of centripetal force acting on the particle to its linear momentum is given by,
questions.solutionText: Solution When a particle executes a uniform circular motion, then its speed remains constant. Let the mass of the particle be m and let its speed be v . Linear momentum is given by, p = m v . ∴ v = p m . . . 1 Centripetal force is given by, F = m v 2 r ⇒ F = m × p m 2 r = p 2 m r . . . 2 . Hence, the ratio of centripetal force to its linear momentum is, F p = p 2 m r p = p m r ⇒ F p = v r [using equation 1 ] Hence, the required ratio is, v r .
questions.solution: Solution When a particle executes a uniform circular motion, then its speed remains constant. Let the mass of the particle be m and let its speed be v . Linear momentum is given by, p = m v . ∴ v = p m . . . 1 Centripetal force is given by, F = m v 2 r ⇒ F = m × p m 2 r = p 2 m r . . . 2 . Hence, the ratio of centripetal force to its linear momentum is, F p = p 2 m r p = p m r ⇒ F p = v r [using equation 1 ] Hence, the required ratio is, v r .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

When a particle executes a uniform circular motion, then its speed remains constant.

Let the mass of the particle be m and let its speed be v.

Linear momentum is given by, p=mv.

∴ v=pm ...1

Centripetal force is given by,

F=mv2r

⇒ F=m×pm2r=p2mr ...2.

Hence, the ratio of centripetal force to its linear momentum is,

Fp=p2mrp=pmr

⇒ Fp=vr [using equation 1]

Hence, the required ratio is, vr.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Among the following, the equation which represents a progressive wave is
questions.questionText: Among the following, the equation which represents a progressive wave is
questions.question: Among the following, the equation which represents a progressive wave is
questions.prompt: Among the following, the equation which represents a progressive wave is
questions.html: Among the following, the equation which represents a progressive wave is
questions.questionHtml: Among the following, the equation which represents a progressive wave is
questions.solutionText: Solution A progressive (travelling) wave must be a function of ( a x ± b t ) , i.e., both x and t must appear together in a single argument. (1) y = A cos ⁡ a x sin ⁡ b t → x and t are separated ⇒ stationary wave (2) y = A sin ⁡ b t → no x dependence ⇒ not a wave (3) y = A cos ⁡ ( a x + b t ) → x and t together, bounded function ⇒ progressive wave (4) y = A tan ⁡ ( a x + b t ) → argument is ( a x + b t ) but tan is unbounded and discontinuous ⇒ cannot represent a physical wave Correct option: (3)
questions.solution: Solution A progressive (travelling) wave must be a function of ( a x ± b t ) , i.e., both x and t must appear together in a single argument. (1) y = A cos ⁡ a x sin ⁡ b t → x and t are separated ⇒ stationary wave (2) y = A sin ⁡ b t → no x dependence ⇒ not a wave (3) y = A cos ⁡ ( a x + b t ) → x and t together, bounded function ⇒ progressive wave (4) y = A tan ⁡ ( a x + b t ) → argument is ( a x + b t ) but tan is unbounded and discontinuous ⇒ cannot represent a physical wave Correct option: (3)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
A progressive (travelling) wave must be a function of (ax±bt), i.e., both x and t must appear together in a single argument.

(1) y=Acos⁡axsin⁡bt → x and t are separated ⇒ stationary wave
(2) y=Asin⁡bt → no x dependence ⇒ not a wave
(3) y=Acos⁡(ax+bt) → x and t together, bounded function ⇒ progressive wave
(4) y=Atan⁡(ax+bt) → argument is (ax+bt) but tan is unbounded and discontinuous ⇒ cannot represent a physical wave

Correct option: (3)

questions.text: At what distance from the earth's centre, the intensity of gravitational field due to the earth and moon will be zero? The masses of the earth and the moon are 5.98 × 10 24 k g a n d 7.35 × 10 22 k g respectively and the distance between the earth and the moon is 3 .85 × 10 8 m .

questions.questionHtml: At what distance from the earth's centre, the intensity of gravitational field due to the earth and moon will be zero? The masses of the earth and the moon are 5.98×1024 kg and 7.35×1022 kg respectively and the distance between the earth and the moon is 3.85×108 m.
questions.solutionText: Solution Let the point be at distance x from Earth's centre. Then distance from Moon = ( 3.85 × 10 8 − x ) . For zero net field: G M E x 2 = G M M ( 3.85 × 10 8 − x ) 2 x 2 ( 3.85 × 10 8 − x ) 2 = M E M M = 5.98 × 10 24 7.35 × 10 22 = 598 7.35 ≈ 81.36 x 3.85 × 10 8 − x = 81.36 ≈ 9.02 x = 9.02 ( 3.85 × 10 8 − x ) x = 34.73 × 10 8 − 9.02 x 10.02 x = 34.73 × 10 8 x = 34.73 × 10 8 10.02 ≈ 3.47 × 10 8 m Correct option: (1) 3.47 × 10 8 m
questions.solution: Solution Let the point be at distance x from Earth's centre. Then distance from Moon = ( 3.85 × 10 8 − x ) . For zero net field: G M E x 2 = G M M ( 3.85 × 10 8 − x ) 2 x 2 ( 3.85 × 10 8 − x ) 2 = M E M M = 5.98 × 10 24 7.35 × 10 22 = 598 7.35 ≈ 81.36 x 3.85 × 10 8 − x = 81.36 ≈ 9.02 x = 9.02 ( 3.85 × 10 8 − x ) x = 34.73 × 10 8 − 9.02 x 10.02 x = 34.73 × 10 8 x = 34.73 × 10 8 10.02 ≈ 3.47 × 10 8 m Correct option: (1) 3.47 × 10 8 m
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Let the point be at distance x from Earth's centre. Then distance from Moon =(3.85×108−x).

For zero net field:
GMEx2=GMM(3.85×108−x)2

x2(3.85×108−x)2=MEMM=5.98×10247.35×1022=5987.35≈81.36

x3.85×108−x=81.36≈9.02

x=9.02(3.85×108−x)
x=34.73×108−9.02x
10.02x=34.73×108
x=34.73×10810.02≈3.47×108 m

Correct option: (1) 3.47×108 m

questions.solutionText: Solution Total energy in SHM: E = 1 2 k A 2 The energy depends only on spring constant k and amplitude A , not on mass. Since k and A remain the same, doubling the mass does not change E . New energy = E Correct option: (1) E
questions.solution: Solution Total energy in SHM: E = 1 2 k A 2 The energy depends only on spring constant k and amplitude A , not on mass. Since k and A remain the same, doubling the mass does not change E . New energy = E Correct option: (1) E

Solution
Total energy in SHM: E=12kA2

The energy depends only on spring constant k and amplitude A, not on mass.

Since k and A remain the same, doubling the mass does not change E.

New energy =E

Correct option: (1) E

questions.text: If the mass of neutron = 1.7 × 10 - 27 kg , then the de-Broglie wavelength of neutron of energy 3 eV is ( h = 6.6 × 10 - 34 J - s )

questions.prompt: If the mass of neutron = 1.7 × 10 - 27 kg , then the de-Broglie wavelength of neutron of energy 3 eV is ( h = 6.6 × 10 - 34 J - s )
questions.html: If the mass of neutron =1.7×10-27 kg, then the de-Broglie wavelength of neutron of energy 3 eV is (h=6.6×10-34 J-s)
questions.questionHtml: If the mass of neutron =1.7×10-27 kg, then the de-Broglie wavelength of neutron of energy 3 eV is (h=6.6×10-34 J-s)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
λ=h2mE

E=3 eV=3×1.6×10−19=4.8×10−19 J

2mE=2×1.7×10−27×4.8×10−19=16.32×10−46=1.632×10−45

2mE=1.632×10−45≈4.04×10−23

λ=6.6×10−344.04×10−23≈1.6×10−11 m

Correct option: (2) 1.6×10−11 m

questions.text: A body covers first 1 3 part of its journey with a velocity of 2 m s - 1 , next 1 3 part with a velocity of 3 m s - 1 and the rest of the journey with a velocity 6 m s - 1 . The average velocity of the body will be
questions.questionText: A body covers first 1 3 part of its journey with a velocity of 2 m s - 1 , next 1 3 part with a velocity of 3 m s - 1 and the rest of the journey with a velocity 6 m s - 1 . The average velocity of the body will be

questions.html: A body covers first 13 part of its journey with a velocity of 2 m s-1, next 13 part with a velocity of 3 m s-1 and the rest of the journey with a velocity 6 m s-1. The average velocity of the body will be
questions.questionHtml: A body covers first 13 part of its journey with a velocity of 2 m s-1, next 13 part with a velocity of 3 m s-1 and the rest of the journey with a velocity 6 m s-1. The average velocity of the body will be
questions.solutionText: Solution Let the total distance be 3 x m . For first 1 3 rd distance x m , let time be t 1 . ∵ time = distance speed ∴ t 1 = x 2 s . . . ( i ) For second 1 3 rd distance x m , let time be t 2 . ∵ time = distance speed ∴ t 2 = x 3 s . . . ( ii ) and for third 1 3 rd distance x m , let time be t 3 . ⇒ t 3 = x 6 . . . ( iii ) ∴ Average velocity for entire journey. v av = total displacement total time ⇒ v av = 3 x t 1 + t 2 + t 3 = 3 x x 2 + x 3 + x 6 = 3 x x 3 + 2 + 1 6 = 3 m s - 1
questions.solution: Solution Let the total distance be 3 x m . For first 1 3 rd distance x m , let time be t 1 . ∵ time = distance speed ∴ t 1 = x 2 s . . . ( i ) For second 1 3 rd distance x m , let time be t 2 . ∵ time = distance speed ∴ t 2 = x 3 s . . . ( ii ) and for third 1 3 rd distance x m , let time be t 3 . ⇒ t 3 = x 6 . . . ( iii ) ∴ Average velocity for entire journey. v av = total displacement total time ⇒ v av = 3 x t 1 + t 2 + t 3 = 3 x x 2 + x 3 + x 6 = 3 x x 3 + 2 + 1 6 = 3 m s - 1
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Let the total distance be 3x m.


For first 13rd distance x m, let time be t1.

∵ time=distancespeed

∴t1=x2 s ...(i)

For second 13rd distance x m, let time be t2.

∵ time=distancespeed

∴t2=x3 s ...(ii)

and for third 13rd distance x m, let time be t3.

⇒t3=x6 ...(iii)

∴ Average velocity for entire journey.

vav=total displacementtotal time

⇒vav=3xt1+t2+t3

=3xx2+x3+x6

=3xx3+2+16=3 m s-1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Magnetic moment of bar magnet is M . The work done to change the alignment from parallel to perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field B will be
questions.questionText: Magnetic moment of bar magnet is M . The work done to change the alignment from parallel to perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field B will be
questions.question: Magnetic moment of bar magnet is M . The work done to change the alignment from parallel to perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field B will be
questions.prompt: Magnetic moment of bar magnet is M . The work done to change the alignment from parallel to perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field B will be
questions.html: Magnetic moment of bar magnet is M. The work done to change the alignment from parallel to perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field B will be
questions.questionHtml: Magnetic moment of bar magnet is M. The work done to change the alignment from parallel to perpendicular to the direction of the magnetic field B will be
questions.solutionText: Solution Work done in rotating a bar magnet of magnetic moment M in a uniform magnetic field B is given by W = ∆ U = U 2 - U 1 ⇒ W = - M B cos θ 2 - - M B cos θ 1 ∵ U = - M B cos θ ⇒ W = M B cos θ 1 - cos θ 2 ...(i) Now given, θ 1 = 0 ° (as magnet is in initially parallel to the magnetic field) & θ 2 = 90 ° ∴ Putting in (i), We get W = M B cos 0 ° - cos 90 ° ⇒ W = M B
questions.solution: Solution Work done in rotating a bar magnet of magnetic moment M in a uniform magnetic field B is given by W = ∆ U = U 2 - U 1 ⇒ W = - M B cos θ 2 - - M B cos θ 1 ∵ U = - M B cos θ ⇒ W = M B cos θ 1 - cos θ 2 ...(i) Now given, θ 1 = 0 ° (as magnet is in initially parallel to the magnetic field) & θ 2 = 90 ° ∴ Putting in (i), We get W = M B cos 0 ° - cos 90 ° ⇒ W = M B
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Work done in rotating a bar magnet of magnetic moment M in a uniform magnetic field B is given by

W=∆U=U2-U1

⇒W=-MBcosθ2--MBcosθ1

∵U=-MBcosθ

⇒W=MBcosθ1-cosθ2...(i)

Now given,

θ1=0° (as magnet is in initially parallel to the magnetic field)

& θ2=90°

∴ Putting in (i), We get

W=MBcos0°-cos90°

⇒W=MB

questions.text: A metallic scale reads correctly at θ 1 . At θ ∘ C , the correct reading will be [ α = coefficient of linear expansion ]
questions.questionText: A metallic scale reads correctly at θ 1 . At θ ∘ C , the correct reading will be [ α = coefficient of linear expansion ]
questions.question: A metallic scale reads correctly at θ 1 . At θ ∘ C , the correct reading will be [ α = coefficient of linear expansion ]
questions.prompt: A metallic scale reads correctly at θ 1 . At θ ∘ C , the correct reading will be [ α = coefficient of linear expansion ]
questions.html: A metallic scale reads correctly at θ1. At θ∘C, the correct reading will be [α= coefficient of linear expansion ]
questions.questionHtml: A metallic scale reads correctly at θ1. At θ∘C, the correct reading will be [α= coefficient of linear expansion ]
questions.solutionText: Solution The scale is calibrated at θ 1 . At temperature θ , each division of the scale expands by a factor [ 1 + α ( θ − θ 1 ) ] . So the actual length corresponding to a scale reading is larger than what the scale shows. Correct reading = scale reading × [ 1 + α ( θ − θ 1 ) ] Correct option: (3) ^
questions.solution: Solution The scale is calibrated at θ 1 . At temperature θ , each division of the scale expands by a factor [ 1 + α ( θ − θ 1 ) ] . So the actual length corresponding to a scale reading is larger than what the scale shows. Correct reading = scale reading × [ 1 + α ( θ − θ 1 ) ] Correct option: (3) ^
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The scale is calibrated at θ1. At temperature θ, each division of the scale expands by a factor [1+α(θ−θ1)].

So the actual length corresponding to a scale reading is larger than what the scale shows.

Correct reading = scale reading ×[1+α(θ−θ1)]

Correct option: (3)
^

γ=CpCv=1.67≈53

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: For regular shapes of the small body, which of the following statement is true?
questions.questionText: For regular shapes of the small body, which of the following statement is true?
questions.question: For regular shapes of the small body, which of the following statement is true?
questions.prompt: For regular shapes of the small body, which of the following statement is true?
questions.html: For regular shapes of the small body, which of the following statement is true?
questions.questionHtml: For regular shapes of the small body, which of the following statement is true?
questions.solutionText: Solution Centre of mass is a point where the distribution of mass is equal around that point and it is not dependent on the gravitational field. On the other hand, the centre of gravity is a point at which distribution of weight of body is equal in all direction and this term is dependent on the gravitational field. For a special case where gravitational field is uniform, the centre of mass and centre of gravity is coincides. For a regular shaped and small body, the gravitational field can be assumed to be uniform, hence, the centre of mass coincides with centre of gravity. .
questions.solution: Solution Centre of mass is a point where the distribution of mass is equal around that point and it is not dependent on the gravitational field. On the other hand, the centre of gravity is a point at which distribution of weight of body is equal in all direction and this term is dependent on the gravitational field. For a special case where gravitational field is uniform, the centre of mass and centre of gravity is coincides. For a regular shaped and small body, the gravitational field can be assumed to be uniform, hence, the centre of mass coincides with centre of gravity. .
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Centre of mass is a point where the distribution of mass is equal around that point and it is not dependent on the gravitational field. On the other hand, the centre of gravity is a point at which distribution of weight of body is equal in all direction and this term is dependent on the gravitational field.

For a special case where gravitational field is uniform, the centre of mass and centre of gravity is coincides. For a regular shaped and small body, the gravitational field can be assumed to be uniform, hence, the centre of mass coincides with centre of gravity.


.

questions.text: A liquid drop having surface energy E is spread into 512 droplets of the same size. The final surface energy of the droplets is
questions.questionText: A liquid drop having surface energy E is spread into 512 droplets of the same size. The final surface energy of the droplets is
questions.question: A liquid drop having surface energy E is spread into 512 droplets of the same size. The final surface energy of the droplets is
questions.prompt: A liquid drop having surface energy E is spread into 512 droplets of the same size. The final surface energy of the droplets is
questions.html: A liquid drop having surface energy E is spread into 512 droplets of the same size. The final surface energy of the droplets is
questions.questionHtml: A liquid drop having surface energy E is spread into 512 droplets of the same size. The final surface energy of the droplets is
questions.solutionText: Solution The surface area of the liquid drop is A = 4 π R 2 Its surface energy is E When the drop splits in 512 droplets, the surface area of each droplet is 4 π r 2 ∴ Total surface area A 2 = 512 × 4 π r 2 The volume of the bigger drop is 4 3 π R 3 and the volume of small droplets is 512 × 4 3 π r 3 ∴ 4 3 π R 3 = 512 × 4 3 π r 3 ⇒ r = R 8 ∴ A 2 = 512 × 4 π r 2 = 512 × 4 π R 8 2 = 8 A 1 Surface energy E = A . T ( T is surface tension and A is area) ∴ E n E 0 = A 2 ⋅ T A 1 ⋅ T = 8 ⋅ A 1 A 1 = 8 ∴ E n = 8 E ;
questions.solution: Solution The surface area of the liquid drop is A = 4 π R 2 Its surface energy is E When the drop splits in 512 droplets, the surface area of each droplet is 4 π r 2 ∴ Total surface area A 2 = 512 × 4 π r 2 The volume of the bigger drop is 4 3 π R 3 and the volume of small droplets is 512 × 4 3 π r 3 ∴ 4 3 π R 3 = 512 × 4 3 π r 3 ⇒ r = R 8 ∴ A 2 = 512 × 4 π r 2 = 512 × 4 π R 8 2 = 8 A 1 Surface energy E = A . T ( T is surface tension and A is area) ∴ E n E 0 = A 2 ⋅ T A 1 ⋅ T = 8 ⋅ A 1 A 1 = 8 ∴ E n = 8 E ;
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The surface area of the liquid drop is A=4πR2
Its surface energy is E
When the drop splits in 512 droplets, the surface area of each droplet is 4πr2
∴ Total surface area A2 =512×4πr2
The volume of the bigger drop is 43πR3 and the volume of small droplets is 512×43πr3
∴ 43πR3=512×43πr3⇒r=R8
∴ A2=512×4πr2=512×4πR82=8A1
Surface energy E=A.T (T is surface tension and A is area)
∴ EnE0=A2⋅TA1⋅T=8⋅A1A1=8
∴ En=8E
;

questions.solutionText: Solution From Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, magnitude of average induced emf is given by, ε = - L ∆ i ∆ t , where L , i & t represents coefficient of self inductance, current and time respectively. On substituting corresponding parameters in above relation, we have ⇒ 1 = - L 10 - ( - 10 ) 0 . 5 ⇒ L = 1 × 0 . 5 20 = 1 40 = 0 . 025 H = 25 mH *

On substituting corresponding parameters in above relation, we have ⇒ 1 = - L 10 - ( - 10 ) 0 . 5 ⇒ L = 1 × 0 . 5 20 = 1 40 = 0 . 025 H = 25 mH *
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

From Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, magnitude of average induced emf is given by, ε=-L∆i∆t, where L, i & t represents coefficient of self inductance, current and time respectively.

On substituting corresponding parameters in above relation, we have
⇒1=-L10-(-10)0.5
⇒L=1×0.520=140=0.025 H=25 mH

questions.questionHtml:
A block of mass 2 kg is placed on a smooth wedge of mass 8 kg . The wedge has an angle of inclination of 37∘ and is placed on a perfectly frictionless horizontal floor. A horizontal force F is applied to the wedge such that the 2 kg block remains at rest relative to the wedge (it does not slide up or down the incline). What is the magnitude of the applied force F ?
(Take g=10 m/s2 and sin⁡37∘=3/5 )
questions.solutionText: Solution The block and wedge move together, so treat the system as one unit. Total mass M = 2 + 8 = 10 kg For the 2 kg block to remain stationary on the wedge, the only horizontal force on it is the normal reaction from the wedge surface. Resolving forces on the 2 kg block: N sin ⁡ 37 ° = m a ⇒ horizontal N cos ⁡ 37 ° = m g ⇒ vertical Dividing: tan ⁡ 37 ° = a g a = g tan ⁡ 37 ° = 10 × 3 / 5 4 / 5 = 10 × 3 4 = 7.5 m/s 2 Applying Newton's second law to the entire system: F = ( m + M ) a = 10 × 7.5 = 75 N Correct option: (B) 75 N :
questions.solution: Solution The block and wedge move together, so treat the system as one unit. Total mass M = 2 + 8 = 10 kg For the 2 kg block to remain stationary on the wedge, the only horizontal force on it is the normal reaction from the wedge surface. Resolving forces on the 2 kg block: N sin ⁡ 37 ° = m a ⇒ horizontal N cos ⁡ 37 ° = m g ⇒ vertical Dividing: tan ⁡ 37 ° = a g a = g tan ⁡ 37 ° = 10 × 3 / 5 4 / 5 = 10 × 3 4 = 7.5 m/s 2 Applying Newton's second law to the entire system: F = ( m + M ) a = 10 × 7.5 = 75 N Correct option: (B) 75 N :
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The block and wedge move together, so treat the system as one unit.

Total mass M=2+8=10 kg

For the 2 kg block to remain stationary on the wedge, the only horizontal force on it is the normal reaction from the wedge surface.

Resolving forces on the 2 kg block:
Nsin⁡37°=ma ⇒ horizontal
Ncos⁡37°=mg ⇒ vertical

Dividing: tan⁡37°=ag

a=gtan⁡37°=10×3/54/5=10×34=7.5 m/s2

Applying Newton's second law to the entire system:
F=(m+M)a=10×7.5=75 N

Correct option: (B) 75 N
:

questions.text: Satellite is revolving around the earth. If its radius of orbit is increased to 4 times of the radius of geostationary satellite, what will become its time period?
questions.questionText: Satellite is revolving around the earth. If its radius of orbit is increased to 4 times of the radius of geostationary satellite, what will become its time period?
questions.question: Satellite is revolving around the earth. If its radius of orbit is increased to 4 times of the radius of geostationary satellite, what will become its time period?
questions.prompt: Satellite is revolving around the earth. If its radius of orbit is increased to 4 times of the radius of geostationary satellite, what will become its time period?
questions.html: Satellite is revolving around the earth. If its radius of orbit is increased to 4 times of the radius of geostationary satellite, what will become its time period?
questions.questionHtml: Satellite is revolving around the earth. If its radius of orbit is increased to 4 times of the radius of geostationary satellite, what will become its time period?
questions.solutionText: Solution When the satellite is moving in circular orbit after launching the satellite, then centripetal force should be equal to the gravitational force, F c = F G 1 2 m v 0 2 = G M m r 2 where G , M , m , h are universal gravitational constant, the mass of the planet, the mass of satellite and height where the satellite is projected, ⇒ m v 0 2 r = G M m r 2 ⇒ v 0 2 = G M r Now the time period of the satellite, T = 2 π r v 0 = 2 π r G M r 1 / 2 ⇒ T 2 = 2 π r G M r 1 / 2 2 ⇒ T 2 ∝ r 3 ⇒ T ∝ r 3 / 2 ∴ T 2 T 1 = r 2 r 1 3 / 2 or T 2 = T 1 4 3 / 2 = 8 T 1 = 8 days
questions.solution: Solution When the satellite is moving in circular orbit after launching the satellite, then centripetal force should be equal to the gravitational force, F c = F G 1 2 m v 0 2 = G M m r 2 where G , M , m , h are universal gravitational constant, the mass of the planet, the mass of satellite and height where the satellite is projected, ⇒ m v 0 2 r = G M m r 2 ⇒ v 0 2 = G M r Now the time period of the satellite, T = 2 π r v 0 = 2 π r G M r 1 / 2 ⇒ T 2 = 2 π r G M r 1 / 2 2 ⇒ T 2 ∝ r 3 ⇒ T ∝ r 3 / 2 ∴ T 2 T 1 = r 2 r 1 3 / 2 or T 2 = T 1 4 3 / 2 = 8 T 1 = 8 days
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

When the satellite is moving in circular orbit after launching the satellite, then centripetal force should be equal to the gravitational force,

Fc=FG

12mv02=GMmr2

where G, M, m, h are universal gravitational constant, the mass of the planet, the mass of satellite and height where the satellite is projected,

⇒mv02r=GMmr2

⇒v02=GMr

Now the time period of the satellite,

T=2πrv0=2πrGMr1/2

⇒T2=2πrGMr1/22

⇒T2∝r3

⇒T∝r3/2

∴ T2T1=r2r13/2

or T2=T143/2=8T1=8 days

questions.solutionText: Solution Band-gap is the distance between the valence band of electrons and the conduction band. According to Band Theory of Solids, the greater the band-gap width the less conductive is the material.

questions.solutionText: Solution We have, B → is the resultant magnetic field, H → is magnetic intensity, I → is the intensity of magnetization. We know that, B = μ 0 H + I ∵ I = χ m H ⇒ B = μ 0 H 1 + χ m … (1) also B = μ H … (2) From Eqs. ( 1 ) and ( 2 ) , μ = μ 0 1 + χ m … ( 3 ) Also, μ = μ 0 μ r … ( 4 ) From Eqs. ( 3 ) and ( 4 ) , μ r = 1 + χ m So, the only wrong relation is μ 0 = μ 1 + χ m .

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

We have,

B→ is the resultant magnetic field,

H→ is magnetic intensity,

I→ is the intensity of magnetization.

We know that,

B=μ0H+I
∵ I=χmH
⇒B=μ0H1+χm …(1)
also B=μH …(2)

From Eqs. (1) and (2),
μ=μ01+χm …(3)
Also,

μ=μ0μr …(4)

From Eqs. (3) and (4),
μr=1+χm
So, the only wrong relation is μ0=μ1+χm.

questions.solutionText: Solution According to Gauss's law of gravitation, ∮ g → . d S → = - 4 π G M inside , here g is strength of gravitational field and d S is unit surface area of sphere, inside the sphere no mass is present it means, M i n s i d e = 0 so ∮ g → . d S → = 0 , here d S can not be zero, so strength of gravitational field should be zero. questions.text: A water pump is required to lift 600 kg of water per minute from the bottom a well that is 25 m deep and eject it from a hose with a speed of 50 m / s . What is the average power required by the pump to perform this task? (Take g = 10 m / s 2 )
questions.questionText: A water pump is required to lift 600 kg of water per minute from the bottom a well that is 25 m deep and eject it from a hose with a speed of 50 m / s . What is the average power required by the pump to perform this task? (Take g = 10 m / s 2 )

questions.prompt: A water pump is required to lift 600 kg of water per minute from the bottom a well that is 25 m deep and eject it from a hose with a speed of 50 m / s . What is the average power required by the pump to perform this task? (Take g = 10 m / s 2 )
questions.html:
A water pump is required to lift 600 kg of water per minute from the bottom a well that is 25 m deep and eject it from a hose with a speed of 50 m/s. What is the average power required by the pump to perform this task?
(Take g=10 m/s2 )
questions.questionHtml:
A water pump is required to lift 600 kg of water per minute from the bottom a well that is 25 m deep and eject it from a hose with a speed of 50 m/s. What is the average power required by the pump to perform this task?
(Take g=10 m/s2 )
questions.solutionText: Solution Mass lifted per minute: m = 600 kg, so per second: m ˙ = 10 kg/s Power required to lift water against gravity: P 1 = m ˙ g h = 10 × 10 × 25 = 2500 W Power required to give kinetic energy to water: P 2 = 1 2 m ˙ v 2 = 1 2 × 10 × 50 2 = 12500 W Total average power: P = P 1 + P 2 = 2500 + 12500 = 15000 W = 15 kW Correct option: (C) 15 kW
questions.solution: Solution Mass lifted per minute: m = 600 kg, so per second: m ˙ = 10 kg/s Power required to lift water against gravity: P 1 = m ˙ g h = 10 × 10 × 25 = 2500 W Power required to give kinetic energy to water: P 2 = 1 2 m ˙ v 2 = 1 2 × 10 × 50 2 = 12500 W Total average power: P = P 1 + P 2 = 2500 + 12500 = 15000 W = 15 kW Correct option: (C) 15 kW
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Mass lifted per minute: m=600 kg, so per second: m˙=10 kg/s

Power required to lift water against gravity:
P1=m˙gh=10×10×25=2500 W

Power required to give kinetic energy to water:
P2=12m˙v2=12×10×502=12500 W

Total average power:
P=P1+P2=2500+12500=15000 W =15 kW

Correct option: (C) 15 kW

questions.solutionText: Solution For a hydrogen-like atom, the most probable radius is: r m p = a 0 Z ⋅ n 2 For H e + : Z = 2 , ground state n = 1 , a 0 = 52.9 pm r m p = 52.9 2 × 1 = 26.45 ≈ 26.5 pm Correct option: (3) 26.5
questions.solution: Solution For a hydrogen-like atom, the most probable radius is: r m p = a 0 Z ⋅ n 2 For H e + : Z = 2 , ground state n = 1 , a 0 = 52.9 pm r m p = 52.9 2 × 1 = 26.45 ≈ 26.5 pm Correct option: (3) 26.5
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For a hydrogen-like atom, the most probable radius is:
rmp=a0Z⋅n2

For He+: Z=2, ground state n=1, a0=52.9 pm

rmp=52.92×1=26.45≈26.5 pm

Correct option: (3) 26.5

questions.questionHtml: The size of the following species increases in the order
questions.solutionText: Solution M g 2 + , N a + , F − are isoelectronic (10 electrons each). Among these, higher nuclear charge ⇒ smaller size: Z: M g 2 + (12) > N a + (11) > F − (9) ⇒ size: M g 2 + < N a + < F − A l is a neutral atom with 13 electrons and 13 protons. Neutral atoms are generally larger than their cations but A l has more electrons than the isoelectronic series above and its atomic radius is about 143 pm, which is larger than all three ions. Final order: M g 2 + < N a + < F − < A l Correct option: (1)
questions.solution: Solution M g 2 + , N a + , F − are isoelectronic (10 electrons each). Among these, higher nuclear charge ⇒ smaller size: Z: M g 2 + (12) > N a + (11) > F − (9) ⇒ size: M g 2 + < N a + < F − A l is a neutral atom with 13 electrons and 13 protons. Neutral atoms are generally larger than their cations but A l has more electrons than the isoelectronic series above and its atomic radius is about 143 pm, which is larger than all three ions. Final order: M g 2 + < N a + < F − < A l Correct option: (1)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Mg2+, Na+, F− are isoelectronic (10 electrons each). Among these, higher nuclear charge ⇒ smaller size:
Z: Mg2+(12) > Na+(11) > F−(9)
⇒ size: Mg2+<Na+<F−

Al is a neutral atom with 13 electrons and 13 protons. Neutral atoms are generally larger than their cations but Al has more electrons than the isoelectronic series above and its atomic radius is about 143 pm, which is larger than all three ions.

Final order: Mg2+<Na+<F−<Al

Correct option: (1)

questions.text: Among the following species, identify the isostructural pairs, NF 3 , NO 3 − , BF 3 , H 3 O + , HN 3
questions.questionText: Among the following species, identify the isostructural pairs, NF 3 , NO 3 − , BF 3 , H 3 O + , HN 3

questions.questionHtml: Among the following species, identify the isostructural pairs, NF3,NO3−,BF3,H3O+,HN3
questions.solutionText: Solution Isostructural species have the same shape and the same number of bond pairs and lone pairs. N F 3 : 3 bond pairs, 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal H 3 O + : 3 bond pairs, 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal ⇒ N F 3 and H 3 O + are isostructural N O 3 − : 3 bond pairs, 0 lone pairs → trigonal planar B F 3 : 3 bond pairs, 0 lone pairs → trigonal planar ⇒ N O 3 − and B F 3 are isostructural H N 3 : linear structure, different from all above. Correct option: (3)
questions.solution: Solution Isostructural species have the same shape and the same number of bond pairs and lone pairs. N F 3 : 3 bond pairs, 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal H 3 O + : 3 bond pairs, 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal ⇒ N F 3 and H 3 O + are isostructural N O 3 − : 3 bond pairs, 0 lone pairs → trigonal planar B F 3 : 3 bond pairs, 0 lone pairs → trigonal planar ⇒ N O 3 − and B F 3 are isostructural H N 3 : linear structure, different from all above. Correct option: (3)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Isostructural species have the same shape and the same number of bond pairs and lone pairs.

NF3: 3 bond pairs, 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal
H3O+: 3 bond pairs, 1 lone pair → trigonal pyramidal
⇒ NF3 and H3O+ are isostructural

NO3−: 3 bond pairs, 0 lone pairs → trigonal planar
BF3: 3 bond pairs, 0 lone pairs → trigonal planar
⇒ NO3− and BF3 are isostructural

HN3: linear structure, different from all above.

Correct option: (3)

Which two sets of reactants best represents the amphoteric character of Zn(OH)2 ?
Set I: Zn(OH)2( s) and OH(aq)
Set II: Zn(OH)2( s) and H2O(I)
Set III: Zn (OH)2( s) and H+(aq)

questions.questionHtml:
Which two sets of reactants best represents the amphoteric character of Zn(OH)2 ?
Set I: Zn(OH)2( s) and OH(aq)
Set II: Zn(OH)2( s) and H2O(I)
Set III: Zn (OH)2( s) and H+(aq)
Set IV: Zn(OH)2( s) and NH3(aq)
questions.solutionText: Solution Amphoteric means the substance can act both as an acid and as a base. Set I: Z n ( O H ) 2 + O H − → [ Z n ( O H ) 4 ] 2 − → Z n ( O H ) 2 acts as an acid (accepts O H − , i.e. reacts with base) Set III: Z n ( O H ) 2 + H + → Z n 2 + + H 2 O → Z n ( O H ) 2 acts as a base (reacts with acid) Together, Sets I and III demonstrate both acidic and basic behaviour, confirming amphoteric character. Set II ( H 2 O ) and Set IV ( N H 3 ) do not directly show this dual nature under standard conditions. Correct option: (2) I and III
questions.solution: Solution Amphoteric means the substance can act both as an acid and as a base. Set I: Z n ( O H ) 2 + O H − → [ Z n ( O H ) 4 ] 2 − → Z n ( O H ) 2 acts as an acid (accepts O H − , i.e. reacts with base) Set III: Z n ( O H ) 2 + H + → Z n 2 + + H 2 O → Z n ( O H ) 2 acts as a base (reacts with acid) Together, Sets I and III demonstrate both acidic and basic behaviour, confirming amphoteric character. Set II ( H 2 O ) and Set IV ( N H 3 ) do not directly show this dual nature under standard conditions. Correct option: (2) I and III
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Amphoteric means the substance can act both as an acid and as a base.

Set I: Zn(OH)2+OH−→[Zn(OH)4]2− → Zn(OH)2 acts as an acid (accepts OH−, i.e. reacts with base)

Set III: Zn(OH)2+H+→Zn2++H2O → Zn(OH)2 acts as a base (reacts with acid)



Together, Sets I and III demonstrate both acidic and basic behaviour, confirming amphoteric character.

Set II (H2O) and Set IV (NH3) do not directly show this dual nature under standard conditions.

Correct option: (2) I and III

questions.solutionText: Solution H 2 O 2 has a non-planar structure. The two O − H bonds lie in two different planes that are inclined to each other at a dihedral angle of about 111.5 ° in the gas phase. This geometry resembles a half-open book, where the spine is the O − O bond and the two pages (each carrying one H ) are partially open. Correct option: (4) half open book type structure

questions.solutionText: Solution The substrate is 1-methylcyclohexene. Reaction with B r 2 / H 2 O proceeds via bromonium ion intermediate, followed by anti addition (backside attack by H 2 O as nucleophile). B r 2 forms a bromonium ion on the double bond. Water attacks the more substituted carbon (Markovnikov) from the anti side → O H goes to C1 (more substituted), B r goes to C2 (anti to O H ). The addition is anti, so B r and O H end up on opposite faces (trans to each other). This gives trans-2-bromo-1-methylcyclohexan-1-ol as a racemic mixture (+ enantiomer), which corresponds to C H 3 and O H on the same carbon with B r and H trans on the adjacent carbon. In option (3): C H 3 and O H are on C1 (same carbon), B r is axial/equatorial on C2 trans to O H → consistent with anti addition product. Correct option: (3)
questions.solution: Solution The substrate is 1-methylcyclohexene. Reaction with B r 2 / H 2 O proceeds via bromonium ion intermediate, followed by anti addition (backside attack by H 2 O as nucleophile). B r 2 forms a bromonium ion on the double bond. Water attacks the more substituted carbon (Markovnikov) from the anti side → O H goes to C1 (more substituted), B r goes to C2 (anti to O H ). The addition is anti, so B r and O H end up on opposite faces (trans to each other). This gives trans-2-bromo-1-methylcyclohexan-1-ol as a racemic mixture (+ enantiomer), which corresponds to C H 3 and O H on the same carbon with B r and H trans on the adjacent carbon. In option (3): C H 3 and O H are on C1 (same carbon), B r is axial/equatorial on C2 trans to O H → consistent with anti addition product. Correct option: (3)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
The substrate is 1-methylcyclohexene. Reaction with Br2/H2O proceeds via bromonium ion intermediate, followed by anti addition (backside attack by H2O as nucleophile).

Br2 forms a bromonium ion on the double bond. Water attacks the more substituted carbon (Markovnikov) from the anti side → OH goes to C1 (more substituted), Br goes to C2 (anti to OH).

The addition is anti, so Br and OH end up on opposite faces (trans to each other).

This gives trans-2-bromo-1-methylcyclohexan-1-ol as a racemic mixture (+ enantiomer), which corresponds to CH3 and OH on the same carbon with Br and H trans on the adjacent carbon.

In option (3): CH3 and OH are on C1 (same carbon), Br is axial/equatorial on C2 trans to OH → consistent with anti addition product.

Correct option: (3)

questions.solutionText: Solution Iodoform test is given by compounds with C H 3 C O − group or C H 3 C H ( O H ) − group. (1) C H 3 C H ( O H ) C H 2 C H 3 → has C H 3 C H ( O H ) − → positive (2) I C H 2 C O C H 2 C H 3 → has C H 3 C H 2 C O − , but N a O I halogenates the α -carbon giving positive (3) C H 3 C O O H → carboxylic acid; − C O O H resists iodoform conditions → negative (4) C H 3 C H O → has C H 3 C O − → positive Correct option: (3) C H 3 C O O H
questions.solution: Solution Iodoform test is given by compounds with C H 3 C O − group or C H 3 C H ( O H ) − group. (1) C H 3 C H ( O H ) C H 2 C H 3 → has C H 3 C H ( O H ) − → positive (2) I C H 2 C O C H 2 C H 3 → has C H 3 C H 2 C O − , but N a O I halogenates the α -carbon giving positive (3) C H 3 C O O H → carboxylic acid; − C O O H resists iodoform conditions → negative (4) C H 3 C H O → has C H 3 C O − → positive Correct option: (3) C H 3 C O O H
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Iodoform test is given by compounds with CH3CO− group or CH3CH(OH)− group.

(1) CH3CH(OH)CH2CH3 → has CH3CH(OH)− → positive
(2) ICH2COCH2CH3 → has CH3CH2CO−, but NaOI halogenates the α-carbon giving positive
(3) CH3COOH → carboxylic acid; −COOH resists iodoform conditions → negative
(4) CH3CHO → has CH3CO− → positive

Correct option: (3) CH3COOH

questions.questionHtml: The electronic transitions from n=2 to n=1 will produce shortest wavelength in (where n= principal quantum state)
questions.solutionText: Solution Energy of transition: Δ E = 13.6 Z 2 ( 1 n 1 2 − 1 n 2 2 ) eV For n = 2 → n = 1 : Δ E ∝ Z 2 Shorter wavelength ⇒ higher energy ⇒ higher Z . H : Z = 1 , H e + : Z = 2 , L i 2 + : Z = 3 ( H + is a bare proton with no electrons, so it cannot undergo electronic transitions.) Highest Z = 3 for L i 2 + ⇒ maximum Δ E ⇒ shortest wavelength. Correct option: (1) L i 2 +
questions.solution: Solution Energy of transition: Δ E = 13.6 Z 2 ( 1 n 1 2 − 1 n 2 2 ) eV For n = 2 → n = 1 : Δ E ∝ Z 2 Shorter wavelength ⇒ higher energy ⇒ higher Z . questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Energy of transition: ΔE=13.6Z2(1n12−1n22) eV

For n=2→n=1: ΔE∝Z2

Shorter wavelength ⇒ higher energy ⇒ higher Z.

H: Z=1, He+: Z=2, Li2+: Z=3
(H+ is a bare proton with no electrons, so it cannot undergo electronic transitions.)

Highest Z=3 for Li2+ ⇒ maximum ΔE ⇒ shortest wavelength.

Correct option: (1) Li2+

questions.text: Which of the following sequences correctly represents the increasing order of the ionic radii for the given isoelectronic species?
questions.questionText: Which of the following sequences correctly represents the increasing order of the ionic radii for the given isoelectronic species?
questions.question: Which of the following sequences correctly represents the increasing order of the ionic radii for the given isoelectronic species?
questions.prompt: Which of the following sequences correctly represents the increasing order of the ionic radii for the given isoelectronic species?
questions.html: Which of the following sequences correctly represents the increasing order of the ionic radii for the given isoelectronic species?
questions.questionHtml: Which of the following sequences correctly represents the increasing order of the ionic radii for the given isoelectronic species?
questions.solutionText: Solution All four species M g 2 + , N a + , F − , O 2 − are isoelectronic, each having 10 electrons. For isoelectronic species, greater the nuclear charge, smaller the ionic radius (more protons pull the same electrons closer). Nuclear charges: M g 2 + : Z = 12 N a + : Z = 11 F − : Z = 9 O 2 − : Z = 8 So increasing order of ionic radius (decreasing Z): M g 2 + < N a + < F − < O 2 − Correct option: (A) M g 2 + < N a + < F − < O 2 −
questions.solution: Solution All four species M g 2 + , N a + , F − , O 2 − are isoelectronic, each having 10 electrons. For isoelectronic species, greater the nuclear charge, smaller the ionic radius (more protons pull the same electrons closer). Nuclear charges: M g 2 + : Z = 12 N a + : Z = 11 F − : Z = 9 O 2 − : Z = 8 So increasing order of ionic radius (decreasing Z): M g 2 + < N a + < F − < O 2 − Correct option: (A) M g 2 + < N a + < F − < O 2 −
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
All four species Mg2+, Na+, F−, O2− are isoelectronic, each having 10 electrons.

For isoelectronic species, greater the nuclear charge, smaller the ionic radius (more protons pull the same electrons closer).

Nuclear charges:
Mg2+: Z = 12
Na+: Z = 11
F−: Z = 9
O2−: Z = 8

So increasing order of ionic radius (decreasing Z):
Mg2+<Na+<F−<O2−

Correct option: (A) Mg2+<Na+<F−<O2−

questions.text: The correct order of speed of movement of alkali metal ions in aqueous solution is
questions.questionText: The correct order of speed of movement of alkali metal ions in aqueous solution is
questions.question: The correct order of speed of movement of alkali metal ions in aqueous solution is
questions.prompt: The correct order of speed of movement of alkali metal ions in aqueous solution is
questions.html: The correct order of speed of movement of alkali metal ions in aqueous solution is
questions.questionHtml: The correct order of speed of movement of alkali metal ions in aqueous solution is
questions.solutionText: Solution The alkali metal ions exist as hydrated ions, M H 2 O x + , in the aqueous solution. The degree of hydration, however, decreases with the increasing ionic size as we move from Li + to Cs + . In other words, Li + ion is the most hydrated. e.g. Li H 2 O 6 + . Since the mobility of ion is inversely proportional to the size of their hydrated ions, therefore, amongst the alkali metal ions, lithium has the lowest ionic mobility. Rb + a q > K + a q > Na + a q > Li + a q

Rb + a q > K + a q > Na + a q > Li + a q

Since the mobility of ion is inversely proportional to the size of their hydrated ions, therefore, amongst the alkali metal ions, lithium has the lowest ionic mobility.

questions.solutionText: Solution Solvent extraction is based on the principle of differential solubility. Solvent extraction is based on the principle of differential solubility. The organic compound has a higher affinity for the organic solvent than for water, so it preferentially migrates into the organic layer.

This is governed by the distribution (partition) coefficient: K=solubility in organic solventsolubility in water>1

Correct option: (1)

questions.text: The predominant product formed, when 3-methyl-2-pentene reacts with HOCl , is
questions.questionText: The predominant product formed, when 3-methyl-2-pentene reacts with HOCl , is
questions.question: The predominant product formed, when 3-methyl-2-pentene reacts with HOCl , is
questions.prompt: The predominant product formed, when 3-methyl-2-pentene reacts with HOCl , is
questions.html: The predominant product formed, when 3-methyl-2-pentene reacts with HOCl, is
questions.questionHtml: The predominant product formed, when 3-methyl-2-pentene reacts with HOCl, is
questions.solutionText: Solution 3-methyl-2-pentene: C H 3 − C H = C ( C H 3 ) − C H 2 C H 3 (double bond between C2 and C3, methyl on C3). HOCl adds via electrophilic addition: H + (or C l + as electrophile via chloronium ion) adds to the less substituted carbon (C2), and O H − (nucleophile) attacks the more substituted carbon (C3) — Markovnikov's rule. C l + → C2, O H − → C3 Product: C H 3 C H C l − C ( O H ) ( C H 3 ) − C H 2 C H 3 i.e. O H on C3 (tertiary), C l on C2. This matches option (3): O H on the more substituted carbon (C3 with C H 3 and C H 2 C H 3 ) and C l on C2. Correct option: (3)
questions.solution: Solution 3-methyl-2-pentene: C H 3 − C H = C ( C H 3 ) − C H 2 C H 3 (double bond between C2 and C3, methyl on C3). HOCl adds via electrophilic addition: H + (or C l + as electrophile via chloronium ion) adds to the less substituted carbon (C2), and O H − (nucleophile) attacks the more substituted carbon (C3) — Markovnikov's rule. C l + → C2, O H − → C3 Product: C H 3 C H C l − C ( O H ) ( C H 3 ) − C H 2 C H 3 i.e. O H on C3 (tertiary), C l on C2. This matches option (3): O H on the more substituted carbon (C3 with C H 3 and C H 2 C H 3 ) and C l on C2. Correct option: (3)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
3-methyl-2-pentene: CH3−CH=C(CH3)−CH2CH3 (double bond between C2 and C3, methyl on C3).

HOCl adds via electrophilic addition: H+ (or Cl+ as electrophile via chloronium ion) adds to the less substituted carbon (C2), and OH− (nucleophile) attacks the more substituted carbon (C3) — Markovnikov's rule.

Cl+ → C2, OH− → C3

Product: CH3CHCl−C(OH)(CH3)−CH2CH3
i.e. OH on C3 (tertiary), Cl on C2.

This matches option (3): OH on the more substituted carbon (C3 with CH3 and CH2CH3) and Cl on C2.

Correct option: (3)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Which of the following statements is incorrect with respect to the solubility of a gas?
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is incorrect with respect to the solubility of a gas?
questions.question: Which of the following statements is incorrect with respect to the solubility of a gas?
questions.prompt: Which of the following statements is incorrect with respect to the solubility of a gas?
questions.html: Which of the following statements is incorrect with respect to the solubility of a gas?
questions.questionHtml: Which of the following statements is incorrect with respect to the solubility of a gas?
questions.solutionText: Solution Henry's law: p = K H ⋅ x , where higher K H means lower mole fraction (solubility) at a given pressure. ⇒ Statement (1) is correct. Dissolution of a gas is exothermic ⇒ by Le Chatelier's principle, increasing temperature shifts equilibrium backward, decreasing solubility. ⇒ Statement (3) is correct. By Henry's law, increasing pressure increases solubility. ⇒ Statement (4) is correct. Statement (2) says solubility decreases with increase in both temperature and pressure. The part about pressure is wrong — solubility of a gas increases with increase in pressure. Correct option: (2)
questions.solution: Solution Henry's law: p = K H ⋅ x , where higher K H means lower mole fraction (solubility) at a given pressure. ⇒ Statement (1) is correct. Dissolution of a gas is exothermic ⇒ by Le Chatelier's principle, increasing temperature shifts equilibrium backward, decreasing solubility. ⇒ Statement (3) is correct. By Henry's law, increasing pressure increases solubility. ⇒ Statement (4) is correct. Statement (2) says solubility decreases with increase in both temperature and pressure. The part about pressure is wrong — solubility of a gas increases with increase in pressure. Correct option: (2)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Henry's law: p=KH⋅x, where higher KH means lower mole fraction (solubility) at a given pressure. ⇒ Statement (1) is correct.

Dissolution of a gas is exothermic ⇒ by Le Chatelier's principle, increasing temperature shifts equilibrium backward, decreasing solubility. ⇒ Statement (3) is correct.

By Henry's law, increasing pressure increases solubility. ⇒ Statement (4) is correct.

Statement (2) says solubility decreases with increase in both temperature and pressure. The part about pressure is wrong — solubility of a gas increases with increase in pressure.

Correct option: (2)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.text: Name the gas that can readily decolourise acidified K M n O 4 solution
questions.questionText: Name the gas that can readily decolourise acidified K M n O 4 solution
questions.question: Name the gas that can readily decolourise acidified K M n O 4 solution
questions.prompt: Name the gas that can readily decolourise acidified K M n O 4 solution
questions.html: Name the gas that can readily decolourise acidified KMnO4 solution
questions.questionHtml: Name the gas that can readily decolourise acidified KMnO4 solution
questions.solutionText: Solution Acidified K M n O 4 is a strong oxidising agent (purple colour). It is decolourised by reducing agents. S O 2 acts as a reducing agent: S O 2 + H 2 O → H 2 S O 3 , which reduces K M n O 4 : 2 K M n O 4 + 5 S O 2 + 2 H 2 O → K 2 S O 4 + 2 M n S O 4 + 2 H 2 S O 4 (colourless) N O 2 , P 2 O 5 , C O 2 are oxidising or neutral agents and cannot reduce K M n O 4 . Correct option: (1) S O 2
questions.solution: Solution Acidified K M n O 4 is a strong oxidising agent (purple colour). It is decolourised by reducing agents. S O 2 acts as a reducing agent: S O 2 + H 2 O → H 2 S O 3 , which reduces K M n O 4 : 2 K M n O 4 + 5 S O 2 + 2 H 2 O → K 2 S O 4 + 2 M n S O 4 + 2 H 2 S O 4 (colourless) N O 2 , P 2 O 5 , C O 2 are oxidising or neutral agents and cannot reduce K M n O 4 . Correct option: (1) S O 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Acidified KMnO4 is a strong oxidising agent (purple colour). It is decolourised by reducing agents.

SO2 acts as a reducing agent: SO2+H2O→H2SO3, which reduces KMnO4:
2KMnO4+5SO2+2H2O→K2SO4+2MnSO4+2H2SO4 (colourless)

NO2, P2O5, CO2 are oxidising or neutral agents and cannot reduce KMnO4.

Correct option: (1) SO2

questions.solutionText: Solution Esterification is a relatively slow process at room temperature and does not proceed to completion. Hence, option D is correct.

Identify the major product (P) in the following reaction sequence:
CH3CH2Br→KCN (alc.) (A)→ Conc. H2SO4/H2O,Δ(P)
questions.solutionText: Solution Step 1: C H 3 C H 2 B r reacts with K C N (alc.) via S N 2 , replacing B r − with C N − : C H 3 C H 2 B r → C H 3 C H 2 C N (Propanenitrile) → this is (A) Step 2: C H 3 C H 2 C N treated with Conc. H 2 S O 4 / H 2 O and heat → acid hydrolysis of nitrile gives amide: C H 3 C H 2 C N → C H 3 C H 2 C O N H 2 (Propanamide) Correct option: (C) Propanamide
questions.solution: Solution Step 1: C H 3 C H 2 B r reacts with K C N (alc.) via S N 2 , replacing B r − with C N − : C H 3 C H 2 B r → C H 3 C H 2 C N (Propanenitrile) → this is (A) Step 2: C H 3 C H 2 C N treated with Conc. H 2 S O 4 / H 2 O and heat → acid hydrolysis of nitrile gives amide: C H 3 C H 2 C N → C H 3 C H 2 C O N H 2 (Propanamide) Correct option: (C) Propanamide
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Step 1: CH3CH2Br reacts with KCN (alc.) via SN2, replacing Br− with CN−:
CH3CH2Br→CH3CH2CN (Propanenitrile) → this is (A)

Step 2: CH3CH2CN treated with Conc. H2SO4/H2O and heat → acid hydrolysis of nitrile gives amide:
CH3CH2CN→CH3CH2CONH2 (Propanamide)

Correct option: (C) Propanamide

questions.solutionText: Solution The word "perfunctory" means (of an action or gesture) carried out with a minimum of effort or reflection. "Superficial" means existing or occurring at or on the surface; lacking in depth or thoroughness. Both words imply a lack of depth or thoroughness, making "superficial" the correct synonym.

questions.solutionText: Solution The sentence refers to delaying the proposal until the next meeting. put off → to postpone or delay put out → to extinguish or inconvenience call off → to cancel entirely set up → to arrange or establish The key context is delay until the next meeting, not cancellation, so call off is ruled out. questions.solutionText: Solution Specious → something that appears correct or convincing on the surface but is actually false or deceptive. Credible → believable and trustworthy (opposite sense) Misleading → giving a false impression; deceptive (closest in meaning) Transparent → open and honest (opposite sense) Honest → truthful (opposite sense) The context supports this: the explanation seemed valid but led to an audit, implying it was deceptive. Correct option: (2) Misleading

Correct option: (2) Misleading
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Specious → something that appears correct or convincing on the surface but is actually false or deceptive.

Credible → believable and trustworthy (opposite sense)
Misleading → giving a false impression; deceptive (closest in meaning)
Transparent → open and honest (opposite sense)
Honest → truthful (opposite sense)

The context supports this: the explanation seemed valid but led to an audit, implying it was deceptive.

Correct option: (2) Misleading

questions.text: Find the time between 3 and 4 will the hands of a watch point in the opposite direction?
questions.questionText: Find the time between 3 and 4 will the hands of a watch point in the opposite direction?
questions.question: Find the time between 3 and 4 will the hands of a watch point in the opposite direction?
questions.prompt: Find the time between 3 and 4 will the hands of a watch point in the opposite direction?
questions.html: Find the time between 3 and 4 will the hands of a watch point in the opposite direction?
questions.questionHtml: Find the time between 3 and 4 will the hands of a watch point in the opposite direction?
questions.solutionText: Solution For hands to point in opposite directions, the angle between them must be 180 ° , i.e. they are 30 minute spaces apart. At 3:00, the minute hand is at 0 and the hour hand is at 15 minute spaces. Let the minute hand travel x minutes from 12. The hour hand moves at 1 12 the speed of the minute hand. For opposite direction: minute hand must be 30 spaces behind or ahead of the hour hand. x − ( 15 + x 12 ) = 30 x − x 12 = 45 11 x 12 = 45 x = 540 11 = 49 1 11 minutes past 3 Correct option: (1) 49 1 11 min past 3
questions.solution: Solution For hands to point in opposite directions, the angle between them must be 180 ° , i.e. they are 30 minute spaces apart. At 3:00, the minute hand is at 0 and the hour hand is at 15 minute spaces. Let the minute hand travel x minutes from 12. The hour hand moves at 1 12 the speed of the minute hand. For opposite direction: minute hand must be 30 spaces behind or ahead of the hour hand. x − ( 15 + x 12 ) = 30 x − x 12 = 45 11 x 12 = 45 x = 540 11 = 49 1 11 minutes past 3 Correct option: (1) 49 1 11 min past 3
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
For hands to point in opposite directions, the angle between them must be 180°, i.e. they are 30 minute spaces apart.

At 3:00, the minute hand is at 0 and the hour hand is at 15 minute spaces.

Let the minute hand travel x minutes from 12. The hour hand moves at 112 the speed of the minute hand.

For opposite direction: minute hand must be 30 spaces behind or ahead of the hour hand.

x−(15+x12)=30
x−x12=45
11x12=45
x=54011=49111 minutes past 3

Correct option: (1) 49111 min past 3

questions.solutionText: Solution Pattern: number in middle cell = product of numbers in left and right cells. Letter in middle = letter midway between left and right letters. Row 1: 3 × 8 = 24 → yes. Letters: C and E → midpoint = D → 24D Row 2: 7 × 3 = 21 → yes. Letters: I and M → midpoint = K → 21K Row 3: 4 × 7 = 28 . Letters: D and J → D=4th, J=10th → midpoint = 7th = G → 28G Correct option: (2) 28G

questions.text: Read the following information carefully and answer the question that follows. Six friends-P, Q, R, S, T, and U-are sitting in a straight line facing North. Each of them scored a different number of marks in a mock test: 45,52,68,75,81, and 91 . Use the following clues to determine their seating arrangement and respective marks: 1. S scored a perfect square number of marks and sits third to the right of Q . 2. Q sits at one of the extreme ends of the line. 3. The sum of the marks scored by Q and T is 120 . 4. R scored exactly 16 marks less than U . 5. U sits to the immediate right of S . 6. The person who scored the lowest marks sits to the immediate left of the person who scored the highest marks. What is the absolute difference between the marks scored by the person sitting at the extreme right end and the person sitting second to the left of S?

Read the following information carefully and answer the question that follows.
Six friends-P, Q, R, S, T, and U-are sitting in a straight line facing North. Each of them scored a different number of marks in a mock test: 45,52,68,75,81, and 91 .

Use the following clues to determine their seating arrangement and respective marks:
1. S scored a perfect square number of marks and sits third to the right of Q.
2. Q sits at one of the extreme ends of the line.
3. The sum of the marks scored by Q and T is 120 .
4. R scored exactly 16 marks less than U.
5. U sits to the immediate right of S.
6. The person who scored the lowest marks sits to the immediate left of the person who scored the highest marks.

What is the absolute difference between the marks scored by the person sitting at the extreme right end and the person sitting second to the left of S?
questions.questionHtml:
Read the following information carefully and answer the question that follows.
Six friends-P, Q, R, S, T, and U-are sitting in a straight line facing North. Each of them scored a different number of marks in a mock test: 45,52,68,75,81, and 91 .

Use the following clues to determine their seating arrangement and respective marks:
1. S scored a perfect square number of marks and sits third to the right of Q.
2. Q sits at one of the extreme ends of the line.
3. The sum of the marks scored by Q and T is 120 .
4. R scored exactly 16 marks less than U.
5. U sits to the immediate right of S.
6. The person who scored the lowest marks sits to the immediate left of the person who scored the highest marks.

What is the absolute difference between the marks scored by the person sitting at the extreme right end and the person sitting second to the left of S?
questions.solutionText: Solution From clue 1: S = 81 (only perfect square). Q at extreme end, S third to right → Q at pos 1, S at pos 4. From clue 5: U at pos 5. From clue 4: U − R = 16 → U = 68, R = 52. From clue 3: Q + T = 120. With remaining marks {45, 75, 91}: Q = 75, T = 45, P = 91. From clue 6: lowest(45 = T) immediately left of highest(91 = P) → T at pos 2, P at pos 3. Final arrangement: Q(75,1), T(45,2), P(91,3), S(81,4), U(68,5), R(52,6) Verification: all 6 clues satisfied. Extreme right (pos 6) = R = 52 Second to left of S (pos 4) = pos 2 = T = 45 Absolute difference = | 52 − 45 | = 7 Correct option: B) 7
questions.solution: Solution From clue 1: S = 81 (only perfect square). Q at extreme end, S third to right → Q at pos 1, S at pos 4. From clue 5: U at pos 5. From clue 4: U − R = 16 → U = 68, R = 52. From clue 3: Q + T = 120. With remaining marks {45, 75, 91}: Q = 75, T = 45, P = 91. From clue 6: lowest(45 = T) immediately left of highest(91 = P) → T at pos 2, P at pos 3. Final arrangement: Q(75,1), T(45,2), P(91,3), S(81,4), U(68,5), R(52,6) Verification: all 6 clues satisfied. Extreme right (pos 6) = R = 52 Second to left of S (pos 4) = pos 2 = T = 45 Absolute difference = | 52 − 45 | = 7 Correct option: B) 7
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
From clue 1: S = 81 (only perfect square). Q at extreme end, S third to right → Q at pos 1, S at pos 4.
From clue 5: U at pos 5.
From clue 4: U − R = 16 → U = 68, R = 52.
From clue 3: Q + T = 120. With remaining marks {45, 75, 91}: Q = 75, T = 45, P = 91.
From clue 6: lowest(45 = T) immediately left of highest(91 = P) → T at pos 2, P at pos 3.

Final arrangement:
Q(75,1), T(45,2), P(91,3), S(81,4), U(68,5), R(52,6)

Verification: all 6 clues satisfied.

Extreme right (pos 6) = R = 52
Second to left of S (pos 4) = pos 2 = T = 45
Absolute difference = |52−45|=7

Correct option: B) 7

questions.subject: English & Logical Reasoning
questions.text: A and B are brothers. C and D are sisters A's son is D's brother. How is B related to C
questions.questionText: A and B are brothers. C and D are sisters A's son is D's brother. How is B related to C
questions.question: A and B are brothers. C and D are sisters A's son is D's brother. How is B related to C
questions.prompt: A and B are brothers. C and D are sisters A's son is D's brother. How is B related to C
questions.html: A and B are brothers. C and D are sisters A's son is D's brother. How is B related to C
questions.questionHtml: A and B are brothers. C and D are sisters A's son is D's brother. How is B related to C
questions.solutionText: Solution A and B are brothers. C and D are sisters. A's son is D's brother → A's son is also a brother of C and D, meaning C and D are A's daughters. So A is the father of C and D. B is A's brother → B is the uncle of C. Correct option: (4) Uncle
questions.solution: Solution A and B are brothers. C and D are sisters. A's son is D's brother → A's son is also a brother of C and D, meaning C and D are A's daughters. So A is the father of C and D. B is A's brother → B is the uncle of C. Correct option: (4) Uncle

Solution
A and B are brothers. C and D are sisters. A's son is D's brother → A's son is also a brother of C and D, meaning C and D are A's daughters.

So A is the father of C and D. B is A's brother → B is the uncle of C.

Correct option: (4) Uncle

questions.solutionText: Solution We are given: UNDERSTAND → 1234567823 We need to determine the code for the word START. Step 1: Map letters in "UNDERSTAND" to numbers Letter U N D E R S T A Position 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 The word "UNDERSTAND" has 10 letters, and the code is 1234567823, so we infer the following mapping: U N D E R S T A N D 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 2 3 Step 2: Apply to START START Using the mappings: - S → 6 - T → 7 - A → 8 - R → 5 - T → 7 So, START → 67857

questions.text: Six colleagues-P, Q, R, S, T, and U-are traveling from Delhi to six different cities: Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai, Hyderabad, Bengaluru, and Pune. They use six different modes of transport: Flight, Car, Train, Bus, Bike, and Ship. Here are the conditions: 1. S traveled to Chennai, but not by Ship or Car. 2. The person who went to Mumbai traveled by Flight. 3. U traveled by Bike, but not to Bengaluru or Hyderabad. 4. T traveled to Pune by Bus. 5. P did not travel to Mumbai, and he did not use a Ship. 6. Q traveled to Bengaluru. 7. Delhi is not connected to Kolkata by Ship. Which mode of transport did P use?

Here are the conditions:

2. The person who went to Mumbai traveled by Flight.
3. U traveled by Bike, but not to Bengaluru or Hyderabad.
4. T traveled to Pune by Bus.

questions.questionHtml:
Six colleagues-P, Q, R, S, T, and U-are traveling from Delhi to six different cities: Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai, Hyderabad, Bengaluru, and Pune. They use six different modes of transport: Flight, Car, Train, Bus, Bike, and Ship.

Here are the conditions:
1. S traveled to Chennai, but not by Ship or Car.
2. The person who went to Mumbai traveled by Flight.
3. U traveled by Bike, but not to Bengaluru or Hyderabad.
4. T traveled to Pune by Bus.
5. P did not travel to Mumbai, and he did not use a Ship.
6. Q traveled to Bengaluru.
7. Delhi is not connected to Kolkata by Ship.

Which mode of transport did P use?
questions.solutionText: Solution From the clues: Clue 4: T → Pune, Bus Clue 6: Q → Bengaluru Clue 1: S → Chennai, not Ship, not Car Clue 3: U → Bike, not Bengaluru, not Hyderabad Clue 2: Mumbai person → Flight Clue 5: P → not Mumbai, not Ship Remaining cities for P, Q, R, U: Mumbai, Kolkata, Hyderabad, Bengaluru Q → Bengaluru (clue 6) U → not Bengaluru, not Hyderabad → U goes to Mumbai or Kolkata U travels by Bike, but Mumbai requires Flight (clue 2) → U ≠ Mumbai So U → Kolkata, Bike Remaining cities for P and R: Mumbai and Hyderabad P → not Mumbai (clue 5) → P → Hyderabad R → Mumbai → Flight (clue 2) Remaining transports: Ship, Car, Train, Flight for P, Q, R, S R → Flight S → not Ship, not Car → S → Train P → not Ship (clue 5) → P → Car Q → Ship Check clue 7: Kolkata not by Ship → U goes to Kolkata by Bike. Ship is used by Q (Bengaluru). Consistent. Summary: P → Hyderabad, Car Q → Bengaluru, Ship R → Mumbai, Flight S → Chennai, Train T → Pune, Bus U → Kolkata, Bike Correct option: (3) Car
questions.solution: Solution From the clues: Clue 4: T → Pune, Bus Clue 6: Q → Bengaluru Clue 1: S → Chennai, not Ship, not Car Clue 3: U → Bike, not Bengaluru, not Hyderabad Clue 2: Mumbai person → Flight Clue 5: P → not Mumbai, not Ship Remaining cities for P, Q, R, U: Mumbai, Kolkata, Hyderabad, Bengaluru Q → Bengaluru (clue 6) U → not Bengaluru, not Hyderabad → U goes to Mumbai or Kolkata U travels by Bike, but Mumbai requires Flight (clue 2) → U ≠ Mumbai So U → Kolkata, Bike Remaining cities for P and R: Mumbai and Hyderabad P → not Mumbai (clue 5) → P → Hyderabad R → Mumbai → Flight (clue 2) Remaining transports: Ship, Car, Train, Flight for P, Q, R, S R → Flight S → not Ship, not Car → S → Train P → not Ship (clue 5) → P → Car Q → Ship Check clue 7: Kolkata not by Ship → U goes to Kolkata by Bike. Ship is used by Q (Bengaluru). Consistent. Summary: P → Hyderabad, Car Q → Bengaluru, Ship R → Mumbai, Flight S → Chennai, Train T → Pune, Bus U → Kolkata, Bike Correct option: (3) Car
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
From the clues:
Clue 4: T → Pune, Bus
Clue 6: Q → Bengaluru
Clue 1: S → Chennai, not Ship, not Car
Clue 3: U → Bike, not Bengaluru, not Hyderabad
Clue 2: Mumbai person → Flight
Clue 5: P → not Mumbai, not Ship

Remaining cities for P, Q, R, U: Mumbai, Kolkata, Hyderabad, Bengaluru
Q → Bengaluru (clue 6)
U → not Bengaluru, not Hyderabad → U goes to Mumbai or Kolkata
U travels by Bike, but Mumbai requires Flight (clue 2) → U ≠ Mumbai
So U → Kolkata, Bike

Remaining cities for P and R: Mumbai and Hyderabad
P → not Mumbai (clue 5) → P → Hyderabad
R → Mumbai → Flight (clue 2)

Remaining transports: Ship, Car, Train, Flight for P, Q, R, S
R → Flight
S → not Ship, not Car → S → Train
P → not Ship (clue 5) → P → Car
Q → Ship

Check clue 7: Kolkata not by Ship → U goes to Kolkata by Bike. Ship is used by Q (Bengaluru). Consistent.

Summary:
P → Hyderabad, Car
Q → Bengaluru, Ship
R → Mumbai, Flight
S → Chennai, Train
T → Pune, Bus
U → Kolkata, Bike

Correct option: (3) Car

questions.html: Study the dices given below. What number will be on the face opposite to 6?
questions.questionHtml: Study the dices given below. What number will be on the face opposite to 6?
questions.solutionText: Solution From dice 1: top = 1, front = 2, right = 3 From dice 2: top = 1, front = 5, right = 4 Faces adjacent to 1: 2, 3, 5, 4 → so 6 is opposite to 1 and vice versa. Verification: all faces adjacent to 6 from dice 3 and 4 are 5, 3, 2 and from dice 1 and 2, face 1 is never adjacent to 6, confirming 1 and 6 are opposite. Correct option: (4) 1

questions.text: A dictionary is printed consisting of 7 lettered words only that can be made with a letter of the word CRICKET. If the words are printed at the alphabetical order, as in an ordinary dictionary, then the number of word before the word CRICKET is
questions.questionText: A dictionary is printed consisting of 7 lettered words only that can be made with a letter of the word CRICKET. If the words are printed at the alphabetical order, as in an ordinary dictionary, then the number of word before the word CRICKET is
questions.question: A dictionary is printed consisting of 7 lettered words only that can be made with a letter of the word CRICKET. If the words are printed at the alphabetical order, as in an ordinary dictionary, then the number of word before the word CRICKET is
questions.prompt: A dictionary is printed consisting of 7 lettered words only that can be made with a letter of the word CRICKET. If the words are printed at the alphabetical order, as in an ordinary dictionary, then the number of word before the word CRICKET is
questions.html: A dictionary is printed consisting of 7 lettered words only that can be made with a letter of the word CRICKET. If the words are printed at the alphabetical order, as in an ordinary dictionary, then the number of word before the word CRICKET is
questions.questionHtml: A dictionary is printed consisting of 7 lettered words only that can be made with a letter of the word CRICKET. If the words are printed at the alphabetical order, as in an ordinary dictionary, then the number of word before the word CRICKET is
questions.solutionText: Solution Letters of CRICKET: C(×2), R, I, K, E, T. Alphabetical order: C, E, I, K, R, T Words beginning with C, second letter < R (i.e. C, E, I, K): 4 × 5 ! = 480 Words beginning with CR, third letter < I (i.e. C, E): 2 × 4 ! = 48 Words beginning with CRI, fourth letter < C: none → 0 Words beginning with CRIC, fifth letter < K (i.e. E): 1 × 2 ! = 2 Words beginning with CRICK, sixth letter < E: none → 0 Total = 480 + 48 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 530 Correct option: (1) 530
questions.solution: Solution Letters of CRICKET: C(×2), R, I, K, E, T. Alphabetical order: C, E, I, K, R, T Words beginning with C, second letter < R (i.e. C, E, I, K): 4 × 5 ! = 480 Words beginning with CR, third letter < I (i.e. C, E): 2 × 4 ! = 48 Words beginning with CRI, fourth letter < C: none → 0 Words beginning with CRIC, fifth letter < K (i.e. E): 1 × 2 ! = 2 Words beginning with CRICK, sixth letter < E: none → 0 Total = 480 + 48 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 530 Correct option: (1) 530
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Letters of CRICKET: C(×2), R, I, K, E, T. Alphabetical order: C, E, I, K, R, T

Words beginning with C, second letter < R (i.e. C, E, I, K):
4×5!=480

Words beginning with CR, third letter < I (i.e. C, E):
2×4!=48

Words beginning with CRI, fourth letter < C: none → 0

Words beginning with CRIC, fifth letter < K (i.e. E):
1×2!=2

Words beginning with CRICK, sixth letter < E: none → 0

Total =480+48+0+2+0=530

Correct option: (1) 530

questions.solutionText: Solution Given, ( 1 + x + x 2 + x 3 ) n = ∑ r = 0 3 n a r x r and n is an odd positive integer. ⇒ [ ( 1 + x ) ( 1 + x 2 ) ] n = ∑ r = 0 3 n a r x r ⇒ ( 1 + x ) n ( 1 + x 2 ) n = ∑ r = 0 3 n a r x r If we take n = 1 , ( 1 + x + x 2 + x 3 ) = ∑ r = 0 3 a r x r = a 0 + a 1 x + a 2 x 2 + a 3 x 3 On comparing both sides, a 0 = 1 , a 1 = 1 , a 2 = 1 , a 3 = 1 … ( i ) If we take n = 3 , ( 1 + x ) 3 ( 1 + x 2 ) 3 = ∑ r = 0 9 a r x r ( 1 + x 3 + 3 x 2 + 3 x ) ( 1 + x 6 + 3 x 4 + 3 x 2 ) = ∑ r = 0 9 a r x r ( 1 + x 3 + 3 x 2 + 3 x + x 6 + x 9 + 3 x 8 + 3 x 7 + 3 x 4 + 3 x 7 + 9 x 6 + 9 x 5 + 3 x 2 + 3 x 5 + 9 x 4 + 9 x 3 ) = ∑ r = 0 9 a r x r ( 1 + 3 x + 6 x 2 + 10 x 3 + 12 x 4 + 12 x 5 + 10 x 6 + 6 x 7 + 3 x 8 + x 9 ) = ∑ r = 0 9 a r x r On comparing the coefficient of x on both sides; a 0 = 1 , a 1 = 3 , a 2 = 6 , a 3 = 10 , a 4 = 12 , a 5 = 12 , a 6 = 10 , a 7 = 6 , a 8 = 3 , a 9 = 1 From Eq. (i), we see that, a 0 − a 1 + a 2 − a 3 = 0 , when n = 1 From Eq. (ii), we see that, a 0 − a 1 + a 2 − a 3 + a 4 − a 5 + a 6 − a 7 + a 8 − a 9 = 0 when n = 3 , Similarly, for each odd terms: a 0 − a 1 + a 2 − a 3 + … − a 3 n = 0
questions.solution: Solution Given, ( 1 + x + x 2 + x 3 ) n = ∑ r = 0 3 n a r x r and n is an odd positive integer. ⇒ [ ( 1 + x ) ( 1 + x 2 ) ] n = ∑ r = 0 3 n a r x r ⇒ ( 1 + x ) n ( 1 + x 2 ) n = ∑ r = 0 3 n a r x r If we take n = 1 , ( 1 + x + x 2 + x 3 ) = ∑ r = 0 3 a r x r = a 0 + a 1 x + a 2 x 2 + a 3 x 3 On comparing both sides, a 0 = 1 , a 1 = 1 , a 2 = 1 , a 3 = 1 … ( i ) If we take n = 3 , ( 1 + x ) 3 ( 1 + x 2 ) 3 = ∑ r = 0 9 a r x r ( 1 + x 3 + 3 x 2 + 3 x ) ( 1 + x 6 + 3 x 4 + 3 x 2 ) = ∑ r = 0 9 a r x r ( 1 + x 3 + 3 x 2 + 3 x + x 6 + x 9 + 3 x 8 + 3 x 7 + 3 x 4 + 3 x 7 + 9 x 6 + 9 x 5 + 3 x 2 + 3 x 5 + 9 x 4 + 9 x 3 ) = ∑ r = 0 9 a r x r ( 1 + 3 x + 6 x 2 + 10 x 3 + 12 x 4 + 12 x 5 + 10 x 6 + 6 x 7 + 3 x 8 + x 9 ) = ∑ r = 0 9 a r x r On comparing the coefficient of x on both sides; a 0 = 1 , a 1 = 3 , a 2 = 6 , a 3 = 10 , a 4 = 12 , a 5 = 12 , a 6 = 10 , a 7 = 6 , a 8 = 3 , a 9 = 1 From Eq. (i), we see that, a 0 − a 1 + a 2 − a 3 = 0 , when n = 1 From Eq. (ii), we see that, a 0 − a 1 + a 2 − a 3 + a 4 − a 5 + a 6 − a 7 + a 8 − a 9 = 0 when n = 3 , Similarly, for each odd terms: a 0 − a 1 + a 2 − a 3 + … − a 3 n = 0
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Given, (1+x+x2+x3)n=∑r=03narxr and n is an odd positive integer.
⇒[(1+x)(1+x2)]n=∑r=03narxr⇒(1+x)n(1+x2)n=∑r=03narxr
If we take n=1,
(1+x+x2+x3)=∑r=03arxr=a0+a1x+a2x2+a3x3
On comparing both sides,
a0=1,a1=1,a2=1,a3=1…(i)
If we take n=3,
(1+x)3(1+x2)3=∑r=09arxr(1+x3+3x2+3x)(1+x6+3x4+3x2)=∑r=09arxr(1+x3+3x2+3x+x6+x9+3x8+3x7+3x4+3x7+9x6+9x5+3x2+3x5+9x4+9x3)=∑r=09arxr(1+3x+6x2+10x3+12x4+12x5+10x6+6x7+3x8+x9)=∑r=09arxr
On comparing the coefficient of x on both sides; a0=1,a1=3,a2=6,a3=10,a4=12,a5=12, a6=10,a7=6,a8=3,a9=1
From Eq. (i), we see that,
a0−a1+a2−a3=0, when n=1
From Eq. (ii), we see that,
a0−a1+a2−a3+a4−a5+a6−a7+a8−a9=0
when n=3,
Similarly, for each odd terms:
a0−a1+a2−a3+…−a3n=0
